questions.question: What does prompt caching actually store and reuse?
options: The model's previous answers, so it can repeat them for free
The processed input prefix, so it doesn't have to be reprocessed
A compressed copy of the whole conversation for later search
Your API key and settings, persisted between sessions
questions.explanation: Caching stores processed input (the prefix), not outputs — so identical-prefix requests skip recomputation. It doesn't store answers, search history, or credentials, and it never changes the model's output.

questions.question: Which choice keeps the cache working across turns?
options: Put the current timestamp in the system prompt so the model knows the time
Reorder the tool definitions each request to surface the relevant one
Freeze the system prompt and tools; append the new question at the end
Switch to a larger model partway through for the harder questions
questions.explanation: The cache is an exact prefix match, so stable content must stay byte-identical and volatile content goes last. Interpolating a timestamp, reordering tools, or switching models all invalidate the prefix.

questions.question: Cache writes cost ~1.25× input and reads ~0.1×. When does caching a prefix LOSE money?
options: When the prefix is read back many times within its TTL
When the prefix is written once and never read again before it expires
Whenever the prompt exceeds the model's minimum cacheable length
Whenever you use the default 5-minute TTL instead of the 1-hour one
questions.explanation: A write costs more than base input, so an entry that's written but never read back is pure overhead. Repeated reads are exactly when caching pays off; exceeding the minimum length and the default TTL are not what cause a loss.

questions.question: With Anthropic's computer-use tool, who actually executes the click and type actions?
options: The model executes them directly on your screen
Your application runs each action on a VM and returns a screenshot
Anthropic's servers control your machine remotely
The browser extension runs them with no application code
questions.explanation: The tool is schema-less and the model only requests actions; your harness executes them on a VM/container and feeds back a screenshot. The model has eyes; your code is its hands.

questions.question: For engineers, what is the highest-value use of a browser/UI agent?
options: Filling out long web forms automatically
Scraping data from pages into a spreadsheet
Visual self-verification of UI that has no unit-test oracle
Replacing all unit tests with screenshots
questions.explanation: The killer use is closing the loop where correctness is 'does the rendered UI look/behave right' — a browser becomes the oracle (Phase 3). Form-filling and scraping are minor; screenshots complement, not replace, unit tests.

questions.question: A browser agent logged into your accounts, reading arbitrary pages, with network access — why is that dangerous?
options: Screenshots are large and slow the model down
Browsers cannot be sandboxed, so there is no mitigation
The accessibility tree leaks DOM contents to the model
It has all three legs of the lethal trifecta, so injection can exfiltrate data
questions.explanation: Private-data access + untrusted page content + network egress = the lethal trifecta; an indirect injection can then steal data. Browsers CAN be sandboxed (the mitigation), and the danger isn't latency or the a11y tree.

questions.question: What is a Claude Code plugin?
options: A single skill packaged with a manifest file
One installable unit bundling skills, commands, hooks, subagents, and MCP servers
A marketplace catalog of other people's tools
A settings file that configures your editor theme
questions.explanation: A plugin is a container for many extension types, not one thing — a one-skill plugin is the minimal case. It isn't a marketplace (that's the catalog) or a theme file.

questions.question: In a plugin's directory, where do skills/, hooks/, and agents/ go?
options: At the plugin root, as top-level directories
Inside the .claude-plugin/ directory, next to plugin.json
In a single bundled .zip the marketplace unpacks
In your project's .claude/ directory, referenced by path
questions.explanation: Only plugin.json lives in .claude-plugin/; every component directory sits at the plugin root. Putting them inside .claude-plugin/ is the single most common documented mistake.

questions.question: Why should you treat adding a marketplace like granting shell access?
options: Marketplaces charge your account a fee per plugin install
Adding a marketplace disables your other installed plugins
Plugins can ship hooks and MCP servers that run arbitrary code at your privileges
Marketplaces require you to upload your API key to the catalog
questions.explanation: Plugins are highly trusted: their hooks/MCP servers execute arbitrary code on your machine with your privileges, inside the agent's trusted context. So add only trusted sources and pin to a commit/version — the other options are fabricated behaviors.
